text: BETHLEN, or BEKLAU, a village of Transylvania, in the circle of Belsö-Zolnok.
BETHLEN-SZEUT-MIKLOS, a village of Transylvania, in the circle of Kukullo, 6 m. W of Kökelburg, on the Kökel.

BETHNAL-GREEN, a parish in the Tower division of the hund. of Ossulstone, Middlesex; 21 m. NE by E of St. Paul's. Area 760 acres. Pop. in 1801, 22,310; in 1831, 62,018; in 1841, 74,088. It is within the bills of mortality, and was formerly only a hamlet in the p. of Stepney, from which it was separated in 1740. The inhabitants of this locality are chiefly journeymen silk-weavers, who work in their own houses for the master-weavers in Spitalfields. The manufacture of silks was introduced here, shortly after the revocation of the edict of Nantz, by French refugees, many of whose descendants still exist here. In July, 1838, there were 7,847 looms employed in this parish as follows: viz. 2,144 on velvets; 23 on jacquard velvets; 445 on jacquard or figured goods; and 5,235 on plain goods. The houses are in general built of brick, and often inhabited by several families. Part of the parish is occupied by market-gardens. In the greater part of it the streets are not close; but in one part the pop. is as densely crowded as in the closest and most thickly-peopled parts of the city.
BETHNANG, a village of Wurtemberg, in the Neckar circle, W of Stuttgart.
BETHULIA, a village of Palestine, in the pash. of Acre, N of Nablus.

BETHUNE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais.- The arrond. has an area of 92,279 hectares; and comprises the 8 cantons of B., Cambrin, Carvin, Houdain, Laventie, Lens, Lillers, and Norrent-Fontes. Pop. in 1836, 131,973; in 1846, 136,078.—The cant., comprising 17 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 20,288.—The com. and town, on the Brette, 20 m. NNW of Arras, had a pop. in 1789 of 5,070; in 1836, of 6,805; and in 1846, of 7,150. The chief manufactures of the place are soap, pottery, and beet-root sugar. It has a considerable traffic in lint, cheese, oleaginous seeds, and oil of colzium or colzat, which is facilitated by the canal of the Lave which joins the Lys. B. is the seat of a communal college.—It belonged in early times to the counts of Flanders, but was annexed to France in 1645, when it was strongly fortified by Vauban. In 1710 it was taken by the allies under Marlborough; but it was definitively ceded to France by the treaty of Utrecht.
BETHUNE, a river of France, in the dep. of Seine- Inferieure, rising near Gaillefontaine, and flowing into the Arques, near Arques, after a course of 30 m.
BETIGOLA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Wilna, 9 m. E of Rossiena, near the l. bank of the Dubissa.
BETJUANAS. See BECHUANAS.
BETLER, a village of Hungary, in the com. of Gomör, 8 m. NW of Rosenau, on the Sajo river.
BETLEY, a parish in Staffordshire, 4 m. W of Newcastle-under-Lyne. Area 1,480 acres. Pop. 884.
BETLIS. See Bedles.
BET-MARIAM, a village of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Woojerat, 32 m. S of Antalo.
BETOYES, a tribe of South America, in New Granada, dwelling between the Rio-Meta on the E, the Ariporo on the S, and the Chire on the N. The tribe is numerous, and professes Christianity.
BETPOUEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrenees, cant. of Luz. Pop. 519.
BETRA-PAR, a small low islet, one of the Laccadive group, lying to the N of Peremul-Par, in N lat. 11° 25'.
BETSCHE, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Posen, circle and 11 m. NE of Meseritz. Pop. in 1837, 1,174. The surrounding district is low and marshy. BET
BETSCHKEREK, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Toronthal, on the Bega canal. Pop. of district 9,780.
BETSEY'S ISLE, a small island off the SE coast of Van Diemen's land, at the mouth of the Derwent, in S lat. 43° 4'.
BETSIES, a river in the state of Michigan, U.S., flowing into Lake Michigan, in N lat. 44° 30', after a course of about 50 m. from E to W.
BETTEMBURG, a town of Luxemburg, 6 m. S of Luxemburg, on the r. bank of the Alsette. Pop. 1,180.
BETTENBURG, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Franconia, or the Lower Mayn, NE of Hofheim.
BETTENDORF, a town of Luxemburg, 2 m. E by N of Diekirch, on the river Sure. Pop. 1,594.
BETTENHAUSEN, a town of Hesse-Cassel, in the circle of Cassel, on the Losee. Pop. 1,000. There is a copper foundry here.—Also a town of Saxe- Meiningen, at the foot of Mount Geba. Pop. 570.
BETTERKINDEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Bern, on the Emmen river, near the frontiers of Soluthurn.
BETTESHANGER, a parish in Kent, 4 m. SSW of Sandwich. Area 370 acres. Pop. 18.

BETTIAH, a district and town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, 90 m. NNW of Patna. The district lies between Tirhut on the E, and the Gunduk river on the W; and yields remarkably fine timber. The town is in N lat. 26° 45'.
BETTISCOMBE, a parish in Dorsetshire, 7 m. N by E of Lyme-Regis. Area 1,800 acres. Pop. 53.
BETTISFIELD, a township in the p. of Hammer, in Flintshire. Pop. 370.
BETTLERN, a village of Austria, in Bohemia, SW of Beraun.—Also a village of Prussia, in Silesia, 6 m. SW of Breslau. Pop. 420.
BETTOLA, a village in the duchy of Parma, 20 m. SSW of Placentia, on the Nura.
BETTOLLE, a village of Tuscany, 4 m. E of Asinalunga, in the Val-di-Chiana. Pop. 1,304.
BETTON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Rennes. Pop. 2,021.—Also a township in the p. of Drayton-in-Hales, Salop. Pop. 254.
BETTON'S ISLAND, a small island in the N Pacific, off the NW coast of America, in N lat. 55° 21'.
BETTSVILLE, a village in the state of Ohio, U. S., in Seneca co., 101 m. N of Columbus.

BETTURIAH, a subdivision of the zemindary of Rajshahi, in Bengal, lying along the NE bank of the Ganges. It is low and marshy, and annually inundated over a great portion of its surface, so that it is well-adapted for the cultivation of rice, which is extensively raised.
BETTUS, or Bettws-y-Crwyn, a parish in Shropshire, 7 m. NW of Knighton. Area 8,500 acres. Pop. 452.
BETTWILLER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Rorbach. Pop. 1,123.

BETTWS, a parish in Carmarthenshire, 9 m. NW of Carmarthen. Pop. 1,109.—Also a p. in Glamorganshire, 5 m. N of Bridgend. Pop. 438.—Also a township in Llanfawr p., in Merionethshire, 2 m. NE of Bala. Pop. 232.—Also a parish in Monmouthshire, 3 m. NW of Newport. Area 1,470 acres. Pop. 949.—Also a parish and village in Montgomeryshire, 4 m. N of Newtown. Pop. of p. 821.
BETTWS-ABERGELEY, or Bettws-yn-Rhos, a parish in Denbighshire, 4 m. SW of Abergeley. Pop. 2,661.
BETTWS-BLEDDRWS, a parish in Cardiganshire, 3 m. N of Lampeter. Pop. 227.
BETTWS-CLYRO, a chapelry in the p. of Clyro, Radnorshire, 4 m. N of Hay. Pop. 218.
BETTWS-Y-COED, a parish in Carnarvonshire, 4 m. S of Llanrwst. Pop. 451.
BETTWS-Y-CRWYN. See Bettus.
BETTWS-DISSERTH, a parochial chapelry in Radnorshire, 7 m. WSW of New Radnor. Pop. 132.
BETTWS-EVAN, a parish in Cardiganshire, 5 m. N by W of Newcastle-in-Emlyn. Pop. 426.
BETTWS-GARMON, a parish in Carnarvonshire, 5 m. SE of Carnarvon. Pop. 111.
BETTWS-GWERFYL-GOECH, a parish in Merionethshire, 4 m. NW of Corwen. Pop. 279.
BETTWS-LEIKI, a chapelry in Cardiganshire, 7 m. N by E of Lampeter. Pop. 364.
BETTWS-NEWYDD, a parish in Monmouthshire, 4 m. N by W of Usk. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 106. The Severn is here crossed by a suspension bridge.

BETTWS-YN-RHOS. See BETTWS-ABERGELEY. BETVA, BETWAH, or BETAVA, a river of Hindostan, which rises in Gundwana, near Shapore Ghaut, passes through the Vindhial chain to the S of Chanderi, and after receiving the Jamni, the Desan, and the Birami, falls into the Jumna below Amerapur, after a course of about 350 m.

BETZ, a commune of France, in the dep. of Indreet-Loire, cant. of Pressigny-le-Grand. Pop. 1,296. -Also a cant. and com. in the dep. of Oise, arrond. of Senlis. The cant., comprising 25 com., had a pop. of 8,818 in 1831. The com., 18 m. ESE of Senlis, has a pop. of 650.
BETZDORF, a village of Luxemburg, 12 m. NE of Luxemburg, on the Sive. Pop. 1,366.
BETZENDORF, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, in the circle and 11 m. SSW of Salzwedel, on the Jeetze. Pop. 700.
BETZENSTEIN, or BEZENSTEIN, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, 3 m. from Leopoldstein.
BEUEL, a village of Prussia, on the r. bank of the Rhine, in the district of Bonn. Pop. 425.
BEUERN (NEU), a village of Bavaria, on the Inn, 6 m. from Rosenheim. Pop. 230.
BEUGEN, a town of Holland, in North Brabant, on the l. side of the Maas, 2 m. N of Boxmeer. Pop. 1,437.
BEUGGEN, a village in Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, and on the r. bank of the Rhine, 8 m. E of Lorrach. There is a missionary seminary here.
BEUGURDELEN, a village and castle of Turkey, in Romelia, in the sanjak of Izvornik, 40 m. NE of Izvornik, near the E bank of the Save.
BEUGUY-LE-CHATEAU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Bertincourt. Pop. 809.
BEURE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of Besançon. Pop. 990.

BEUREN, or BEUERN, a town of Baden, in the Middle Rhine circle, SE of Baden. Pop. 1,963.— Also a village of Baden, in the Lake circle, S of Aach. Pop. 171.—Also a town of Wurtemberg, in the Jaxt circle, 7 m. SSE of Nurtingen, at an alt. of 1,182 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,575. There are large distilleries of kirschwasser here.
BEURIE'RES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Arlant. Pop. 1,400.
BEURON, a village of Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen, in the Wald amt. Pop. 70. An ancient abbey of Augustines existed here from its foundation in 1077 till its suppression in 1803.

BEUTELSBACH, a village of Wurtemberg, in the Jaxt circle, on the Beutel, 6 m. W of Schorndorf. Pop. 1,832. The ancestral castle of the royal family of Wurtemberg formerly existed here; but few traces of it remain. BEV

BEUTHEN, or Bytom, a town of Prussian Silesia, in the gov. of Liegnitz, circle and 11 m. ESE of Freistadt, on the l. bank of the Oder. Pop. in 1837, 3,100. It is the cap. of the seignory of Karolath- Beuthen. Pottery and straw-hats are manufactured here, and a considerable quantity of wine is made from the surrounding vineyards.
BEUTHEN (OBER), a town of Prussian Silesia, in the gov. and 50 m. SE of Oppeln, on the Klodnitz. Pop. in 1837, 3,413, of whom 631 were Jews. Iron is made and wrought in the neighbourhood.
BEUVRAIGNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Roye. Pop. 1,215.

BEUVRON, a river of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, rising in the etang of Aron, flowing into the Yonne, on the l. bank, at Clamecy, after a course of 27 m. from S to N. It receives the small affluents of the Champallement, the Corno, the Artet, the Corvol, the Changy, and the Sozay.
BEUVRON-EN-AUGE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Cambremer, 16 m. W of Caen. Pop. 478.
BEUVRY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Cambrin, 2 m. E of Bethune. Pop. 2,764.
BEUVRY-LES-ORCHIES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Orchies. Pop. 2,027.
BEUZEC-CAP-SIZUN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistere, cant. of Pont-Croix. Pop. 1,875.
BEUZEC-CONQ, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistere, cant. of Concarneau. Pop. 1,172.

BEUZEVILLE, a cant. and commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, arrond. of Pont-Audemer.—The cant., comprising 19 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 12,327.—The com., 6 m. W of Pont-Audemer, has a pop. of 2,800. Marble is quarried in the vicinity.
BEVAGNA, a town in the States-of-the-Church, in the deleg. and 20 m. NW of Spoleto, on the Clituno. Pop. 4,000. It occupies the site of the ancient Mevania.

BEVELAND (North and South), two islands of Holland, in the prov. of Zealand, formed by the branches of the Scheldt.—North B., about 6 m. in length, and 4 m. in breadth, lies to the E of the island of Walcheren, from which it is separated by the Sloe. It is formed by the Easter Scheldt; and is separated from South Beveland, and the island of Wolfersdyk, by the Zuid-Vliet. The inundation of 1532 ruined it so completely that for many years nothing was to be seen above water but the tops of the steeples; yet by the gradual deposits of the sea, the surface became so much elevated that the greater part was drained and brought into cultivation in the following century.—The island of South B. is 24 m. long, and from 5 m. to 8 m. broad. It contains the town of Goes, with several villages and forts. It carries on an active trade in corn; and is said to be the most agreeable of all the Zealand islands.—Both North and South B. were occupied by the British in the unfortunate expedition of 1809, but were evacuated in the course of a few weeks.
BEVENAIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Le Grand-Lemps. Pop. 1,064.
BEVENSEN, a town of Hanover, in the principality of Luneburg, 2 m. S of Medingen, on the Ilmenau. Pop. 1,230.
BEVER, a small river of Hanover, which joins the Ems between Bevern and Munster.-Also a river of Prussia, which falls into the Weser at Beverungen.

BEVEREN, a commune of Belgium, in W Flanders, 12 m. S of Furness. Pop. 1,644.—Also a com. and town of Belgium, in the prov. of E Flanders, arrond. and 20 m. NE of Dendermonde, on the line of railway from Antwerp to Saint Nicholas. Pop. 6,368. About 2,000 females are employed in this commune in the manufacture of lace. The other manufactures of the place are thread, tobacco, and leather.
BEVERGERN, a town of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the gov. of Munster, 12 m. NW of Tecklenburg. Pop. 1,000.
BEVERINO, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. of Spezia, on the Vara. Pop. 765.

BEVERLEY, an important borough, market-town and township, within the liberty of B., E. R. of Yorkshire; 183 m. N of London; 29 m. ESE of York; 9 m. NE of Kingston-upon-Hull; and about 1 m. W of the river Hull, with which it is connected by means of a canal. The area of the borough and liberties is 9,160 acres. Pop. in 1801, 6,033; in 1831, 8,302; in 1841, 9,160.—Houses in 1831, 1,805; in 1841, 1,947. -Assessed property in 1815, £33,569; in 1842-3, £42,781. The borough consists of three parishes: St. Mary, St. Nicholas, and St. Martin, with a small portion recently added from the parish of St. John. The liberty of which B. is the head contains the additional townships of Molescroft, Stockhill with Sandholme, Thearne, Ticton with Hullbridge, Weal, Woodmansey with B. park, and part of the township of Aike, all of which are in the parish of St. John. The parishes of St. John, St. Martin, St. Mary, and St. Nicholas, are within the deanery of Harthill, archd. of the E. R. and dio. of York. The town of B. is handsome and well-built. It consists of several streets, the principal one nearly a mile in length, terminating in an ancient gateway. The environs are agreeable, and the air is considered salubrious. The poorer classes of the inhabitants are partially employed in the manufacture of bone-lace. It has a considerable trade in coals, corn, oatmeal, malt, and leather, which is facilitated by a canal called Beverley beck communicating with the river Hull. This cut appears to have been navigable so early as 1344. Several fairs for horses, horned cattle, and sheep, are held annually. Races take place in May on Hurnmeadow, one of the four common pastures belonging to the town. A railway from Beverley to Hull has been proposed.—The borough is divided into two wards, to each of which 9 councillors are assigned. B. is considered as the capital of the east riding of Yorkshire; and the quartersessions for the division are held in the guild-hall. The house-of-correction for the east riding stands at a short distance from the town. The parishes of St. Martin and St. Nicholas, and part of the parish of St. John, unite with B. under the reform act, in returning two members. The pop. of the parliamentary borough in 1841 was 8,409. Electors in 1839-40, 1,053; in 1842-3, 1,122. B. is also the place of nomination, and one of the polling-places, in the election of members for the east riding.—The town of B. originated probably in the monastic establishment of St. John of B. to whom its minster is dedicated. He was born at Harpham-on-the-Wolds, about the year 640, and held the see of York for 33 years, with a reputation for great sanctity. "He was educated," says Fuller, "under Theodorus the Grecian, and archb. of Canterbury. Yet was he not so famous for his teacher as for his scholar, Venerable Bede, who wrote this John's life, which he hath so spiced with miracles that it is of the hottest for a discreet man to digest into his belief." After his death he was canonized by the title of St. John of B.; and his memory was held in such veneration, that when William the Conqueror advanced within 7 m. of the town of B., he ordered that his army should strictly respect the property of the monastery. The original minster was almost entirely destroyed by fire in 1188. The present edifice, supposed to have been completed in the early part of the reign of Henry III., is a ven- BEW erable cruciform structure, with two noble towers at the west end. Notwithstanding the mixture of styles which this structure presents, it is considered equal in purity of composition, correctness of detail, and elegance of execution, to any of the great English cathedrals. The estates of the minster now produce an annual revenue of about £3,000, a portion of which is appropriated to keeping the edifice in repair. The church of St. Mary is an elegant structure, consisting of a nave, transept, chancel, and aisles, with two finely pierced and embattled octagonal turrets. B. is supposed to have been the birth-place of Alfred de Beverley, a monkish historian of the 12th cent. Among the distinguished men who were natives of this place we may mention Dr. John Alcock, bishop of Ely, and founder of Jesus' college, Cambridge; Dr. Fisher, bishop of Rochester, the victim of the intolerance of Henry VIII.; and Dr. John Green, bishop of Lincoln, an elegant scholar, and one of the authors of the 'Athenian letters,' published by the earl of Hardwicke.
BEVERLOO, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Limburg, 12 m. NW of Hasselt. Pop. 1,433.

BEVERLY, a township in Essex co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 16 m. NE of Bolton. Pop. 4,689, chiefly employed in commerce and fishing.— Also the cap. of Randolph co., in the state of Virginia, on the E fork of the Monongahela.—Also a v. in Anson co., in the state of N Carolina.—Also a v. in Washington co., in Ohio.

BEVERN, a town of Brunswick, on the Bever, 3 m. NE of Holzminden. Pop. 1,400. Linens are manufactured and bleached here. In the neighbourhood are the ruins of the ancient castle of Eberstein. -Also a town of Prussian Westphalia, in the gov. of Munster, NW of Warendorf. Pop. 470.
BEVERS, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, on the Inn, in the Upper Engadine, at an alt. of 5,650 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 226.
BEVERSTEDT, a town of Hanover, in the duchy of Bremen, 40 m. S of Stade. Pop. 360.
BEVERSTONE, a parish in Gloucestershire, 2 m. WNW of Tetbury. Area 2,360 acres. Pop. 178.

BEVERUNGEN, a town of Prussian Westphalia, in the gov. of Minden, 8 m. S of Höxter, at the influx of the Bever into the Weser, and on the l. bank of the latter river. Pop. in 1837, 2,017. Oil, brandy, and soap-works form the chief manufactures.
BEVERWYCK DE WYK, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Holland, arrond. and 7 m. N of Haarlem, on the Wykermeir, at the N extremity of the lake of Haarlem. It is of considerable antiquity. Pop. 1,940.
BEVILACQUA, a town of Lombardy, in the prov. and 25 m. SE of Verona, on the Togna, and 5 m. NE of Legnago.
BEVILARD, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, and bailiwick of Moutier. Pop. 882.
BEVILLERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Carnieres. Pop. 1,032.
BEVIO. See STALLA.
BEWALDETH, a township in the p. of Torpenhow, Cumberland. Pop. 73.

BEWCASTLE, a parish in Cumberland, 10 m. N by E of Brampton, and within 6 m. of the Carlisle and Newcastle railroad. It comprises the townships of Bailie, Belbank, and Nixons. Area 26,640 acres. Pop. in 1801, 917; in 1841, 1,274. The rivers Levan and Irthing have their sources in this parish. Coal and lead-ore are found here. B. was anciently a Roman station, and many relics of that people have been found in the neighbourhood. Some vestiges of Bewcastle, a fortress built by Bueth, lord of Gilsland, soon after the Conquest, are yet to be seen here.

BEWDLEY, a borough, market-town, and chapelry, having separate jurisdiction, in the p. of Ribbesford, co. of Worcester, 129 m. NW of London, and 14 m. NNW of Worcester, on the W bank of the Severn. Pop. returned with the p. The chapelry of B. includes the hamlet of Wribbenhall, which, though separated from the borough by the Severn, is connected with it by a handsome bridge, erected in 1797, and, for all the purposes of intercourse and interest, forms a portion of the town. B. was originally included within the marches of Wales; but was united to the co. of Worcester in the reign of Henry VIII. It has been a royal domain since the reign of Henry VII., who erected here a palace for his son Arthur, in which that prince was married by proxy to Catharine of Aragon. The palace suffered much in the parliamentary war, and no traces whatever of the original edifice remain. The town stands on a rising ground on the W bank of the Severn. The houses are in general of a respectable aspect, and the streets are neat, clean, and well-paved. The environs present richly diversified scenery; and the general aspect of the town itself, situated on an eminence, with the houses built at different altitudes, and alternating with trees and gardens, is picturesque. The commerce of B. is chiefly connected with the carryingtrade on the Severn. The tanning of leather is carried on to a considerable extent, and malt is made for exportation. The principal manufacture is that of comb-making; and a manufactory for carpets has been established. This borough was first made a corporate-town by Edward IV. Under the municipal reform act, it is governed by 2 bailiffs, 4 aldermen, and 12 councillors. B., with the parish of Ribbesford, and the hamlets of Wribbenhall, Hoarstone, Blackstone, Netherton, and Lower Mitton with Lickhill, under the reform act, returns one member to parliament. The pop. of the parliamentary borough in 1841 was 7,458. Number of electors in 1837, 400; in 1842-3, 406.
BEWERLEY, a township in the p. of Ripon, N. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. NW of Ripley. Area 5,320 acres. Pop. 1,329.
BEWERSTEDT. See BEVERSTEDT.
BEWICK (OLD and NEW), two townships in the p. of Eglingham, Northumberland, the former 65 m., the latter 7 m. SE of Wooler. Pop. of former 176; of latter 121.
BEWLE. See Beblenheim.
BEWLEY. See BEAULIEU.

BEX, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, in the district and 5 m. SSE of Aigle, and 25 m. SE of Lausanne. It is situated at the base of finely wooded hills, on the r. bank of the Avencon, about 2 m. above the confluence of that river with the Rhone, and at an alt. of 1,450 ft. above sea-level. It is large and substantially built of stone; and possesses sulphureous springs and baths. The surrounding district is exceedingly picturesque, and generally fertile. Extensive salt-mines and brine springs occur here in the valley of La Gryonne. The former were discovered in 1554, and were purchased by the gov. of Berne. Their annual produce is estimated at from 20,000 to 30,000 quintals. The locality affords also marble and sulphur. Pop. 2,854.
BEXAR. See Antonio de Bexar.
BEXBACH, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Pfalz, and district of Waldmohr, on the Blies. It contains a custom-house, and the environs afford considerable quantities of coal. Pop. 600.
BEXHILL, a hundred and parish in Sussex, 6 m. W of Hastings. Area 7,900 acres. Pop. 1,822.
BEXIS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 50 m. NNW of Valencia, and 15 m. WNW of Segorbe. It stands on the summit of a mountain, and was formerly a place of considerable strength. Pop. 1,000. BEY
BEXLEY, a parish in Kent, 3 m. W by S of Dartford, comprising the hamlets of Blendon, Bridgend, and Upton. Area 5,370 acres. Pop. 3,955.
BEXLEY (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of the Arctic ocean, in N lat. 68° 58', W long. 115° 47'.
BEXTON, a township in the p. of Knutsford, Cheshire, 1 m. SSE of Nether-Knutsford. Pop. 77.
BEXWELL, a parish in Norfolk, 1 m. E of Downham-Market. Area 1,280 acres. Pop. 70.
BEYADHYE (EL), a town of Arabia, in the Hedjaz, 40 m. W of Mecca.
BEYAH. See BEAS.
BEYCHAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, and cant. of Carbon-Blanc. Pop. 523.
BEYEDA, a mountain-range of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Samen. It stretches along the S bank of the Tecazze, and reaches the line of perpetual snow.
BEYENBURG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, on the l. bank of the Wipper, 7 m. E of Eberfield. It has manufactories of coarse cotton fabrics and ticking. Pop. 1,000.

BEYERFELD, a village of Saxony, in the Erzegebirge circle, and bail. of Grunhayn, about 2 m. N of Schwarzenberg. It has a considerable trade in nails and white-iron utensils. In the environs are manufactories of vitriol, sulphur, and alum.

BEYERLAND, or BEIERLAND, an island of Holland, in the delta of the Maas, and prov. of Holland. It is separated on the N by the Old Maas from the island of Ysselmonde, on the E from that of Stryen by the Dortschekil, and by the Het Spuy on the W from the island of Voorn. On the S it is bathed by the Hollands Diep. It is about 19 m. in length, and 9 in breadth.

BEYERLAND (NIEUW), a village of Holland, in the island of the same name, on the Spuy, 9 m. SW of Rotterdam. Pop. 1,000. BEYERLAND (OUD), BEIJERLAND, OF HITZERT, a town of Holland, in the island of the same name, on a canal joining the Hollands Diep, 12 m. SSW of Rotterdam. Pop. 3,000.
BEYHAR, or VIHAR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, on the l. bank of the Toresha, 27 m. WNW of Rangamatty, and 60 m. NE of Dinajepur. It is the modern residence of the Cutch-Bahar rajas.
BEYKANEER. See BIKANIR.
BEYKEM, an affluent of the Jenisei, in Mongolia, descending from Mount Shabinai.
BEYLA, a village of Nubia, 30 m. SSW of Teawa, and 80 m. E of Senaar.
BEYLAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Cutch and dist. of Wager, 60 m. NE of Anjar, on the confines of the Runn. It occupies a commanding and important situation.
BEYLAN. See BEILAN.
BEYLAPUR, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 45 m. SW of Aurungabad, on the l. bank of the Para.
BEYLLANY, or BEILLANY, a town of Nubia, in the Wady Nuba, on the l. bank of the Nile, 4 m. SSW of Ipsambol, and 50 m. SW of Derr.
BEYNAC, a cant. and commune of France, in the dep. of Correze, arrond. of Brives. The cant. comprises 6 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 6,847. The village is 10 m. ESE of Brives. Pop. 1,790.
BEYNE-HENSAY, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and 6 m. E of Liege. It possesses manufactories of anchors and anvils. Pop. 1,764.
BEYNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, and cant. of Montfort-l'Amaury. Pop. 1,121.
BEYNUHNEN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of E. Prussia, near the l. bank of the Angerap, 8 m. SW of Darkehmen, and 60 m. SE of Königsberg.
BEYRYS, a village of Upper Egypt, in the Great Oasis, on the caravan route between Darfur and Siut, 47 m. SSE of El Khargeh.
BEYRUSIA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, and pergunnah of Oujein, containing, in 1820, about 300 houses.
BEYSCE, a town of Poland, in the district of Kielce, circle and 27 m. from Miechow.
BEYSSENAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Correze, cant. of Lubersac, 30 m. from Brives. It has some iron-works. Pop. 940.
BEYTON. See BEIGHTON.
BEYTUSH, or BAYTUSH, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Kurdistan, near the N bank of the Tahite, and 40 m. N of Shahrazoor.

BEYTULBARRY, a small district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Berar. It is extremely mountainous, and is, to a great extent, covered with impenetrable forests. The only town of importance which it contains is Ajuntee, situated near the celebrated pass of the same name.

BEZ, a small river of France, in the dep. of Drome, which takes its rise in the mountain of Toussière, passes Châtillon, and falls into the Drome, 6 m. to the S of Die, after a course from E to W of about
BEZ-DE-BELFOURTE, a commune of France, 18 m. in the dep. of Tarn, cant. of Brassac. Pop. 2,039.

BEZAU, or BETZAU, a town of Tyrol, in the circle and 11 m. SE of Bregenz, and near the r. bank of the river of that name. It contains a parish church, and a capuchin convent; and possesses several manufactories of muslin and other cotton fabrics. Pop. 740.
BEZDAN, a town of Hungary, in the comitat and 35 m. NNW of Bacs, and 10 m. NW of Zombor, near the entrance of the canal du Francois into the Danube.
BEZDEJ, or BEZDZIEZ, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 100 m. SE of Grodno, to the SW of Lake Sparovskoi.
BEZDRUDY, a village of Galicia, in the circle and 28 m. WNW of Zloczow. It contains extensive powder-mills.

BEZE, a commune and town of France, in the dep of Côte-d'Or, cant. of Mirabeau. The town is situated near the source of the small river of the same name. It contains extensive manufactories of files, screws, locks, and nails. Pop. 1,031.
BEZENSTEIN. See BETZENSTEIN.
BEZERGHENLI, or BESERGANLU, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Karamania, in the sanjak and 30 m. N of Akserai, and 105 m. NE of Konieh.
